sentences.text: It wasn't easy.
And then, I found out that there are many, many people of different views, that America accepts it.
If you don't work against the government, if you don't try to overthrow the government, you're entitled to your opinions.
And I took a room to take out the child-- she was 2 and 1/2 by that time-- to take her out from the burning city.
And at that time, there were nine communists that were arrested.
But they were let out, I presume, on-- on bail, or something.
And in the barn, there were always something going on.
It was Goldens Bridge in Worcester County.
And Thompson, one of them, was the speaker.
But they wouldn't let me in, the whole community.
But I was curious.
So I left Anita sleeping, asked my landlady to keep and eye on her, and I walked over.
And I heard him talking against the government, that this government has to change, and this government-- he talked.
To me, it was treason.
And then, questions.
And nobody had any-- any questions.
I had to ask a question.
I said, how come you tell me that there is no freedom in the States?
Granted, it's not proved, but you are a accused communist.
You speak here, and I don't see an FBI around you.
Well, I ran from the barn.
They ran after me.
There were all kinds of mixed faiths there.
Ran after me.
I locked myself in the bungalow, called my husband, called my cousin.
They came pronto, fast with a-- with a car, and took me away with the child from there.
They would-- who knows what they would have done to me?
Who knows?
They were so wild, at that time, and just for one question.
I said, by golly, they are no different than Russia.
I'm not allowed to ask a question?
You wanted to know, I guess, about my life in the-- in the underground, the daily life.
It was always stayed in the place of my husband's birth.
He was born in a town called Nalibolki.
Around Nalibolki-- we call it Pushta.
You would call it, here, a jungle.
There are miles and miles of woods and swamps.
People, years ago, used to cut the-- the trees up and ship wood all over Poland and-- for export.
That's why my husband never was locked up, because he went right away to the woods.
He knew everything there.
In this woods, we built a base.
And we built a base in the form of a circle.
In the middle was like a market where, when you're cold, and when you-- when you exercised them, when you came in the morning, the soldiers-- and there was the offices of the commander.
And all around, there were little, like, streets, and huts, and communal bunkers built.
We had, like, streets, actual streets.
And life, as I said before, in the beginning, when we were in the bunkers, close to the city, close to the town, we lived mostly underground.
And we used to go out at night.
And you basically lived like the native Indian.
You tried not to make traces.
Let's say, if you walked in the snow, if you walked forward, you made the steps backward.
And then, you went with a-- with a little twig, or something, and-- and erased it.
And you did a job-- you went to it-- at that time, I only participated in standing guard and cooking.
But when I was with my husband, we used to go out.
When he was sent to do something, like mind the train, or whatever, I was either on the lookout, or I would participate.
You hand one from the other, one from the other.
Usually, was one expert-- the first one.
And then, you handed whatever went from hand to hand, and quietly.
We use to cut telephone poles, so there were no communication.
But the-- the Nazis knew right away when the telephone pole was cut, so they would come running.
So we used to run away.
Basically-- it's a terrible thing to say-- but basically, we were like terrorists.
You ran.
You did, and you ran.
You did, and you ran.
You could never fight like a regular army.
You could just do something, and run.
You could destroy a police station, and run.
You could destroy a certain point that was given to you, and run.
You could make a raid on the Nazis, who went to do an active for the Jews in this and this town, and run.
We could not put a resistance.
We did not have enough support, not from-- not from the aviation, or tanks, or whatever.
We could not put a-- a defense, or whatever, as a standing army.
It was hit, and run.
First of all, the peasants all around will never surrender their ammunition.
If they say collect the ammunition, half of it is buried.
If you find one, and you take from one peasant, he'll tell you that the second neighbor, and the third, and the fourth has it.
Then, the supplies, a lot of supplies, was from Russia too.
And a lot of it was from the Nazis.
We raided, and we got it.
When there was a-- if you-- if you-- we never took prisoners.
That was not our job.
We had no place to keep them, and a prisoner was never taken.
They were killed.
That's it.
I personally couldn't do it, but I couldn't do many things, which I left to the fellows.
Like, let's say, we came to a village-- not a village.
It was like a-- a-- a house on the side, on a-- on a lonely road, like a farm.
And I took a look at the girl, and she wore a blouse out of a prayer shawl.
I saw red.
I ripped it off.
And right here, she had here-- a gun.
That gun was mine.
I ripped it, and there was a gun in her bra.
I never hit anybody in my life.
Her, I hit.
The boys went to the-- to the barn and to the stable and found them full with Jewish bodies.
When the Jewish people were living in the ghetto, or whatever, and passing them by, they would lure them in, rob them, and kill them and bury.
Nothing was left there.
I walked out.
They didn't take a cow.
They didn't take a chicken.
Everything was burned.
Half of them were drunk all the time.
We made our own moonshine.
And-- but you could walk there in the middle of the day.
People could come from the ghetto and from the camps, wherever they were running from.
Nobody touched them.
They brought out bread for them.
I hate to leave a message that, with cruelty and terrorism, you can do something like that.
But there was no other way out.
If you find your father crucified to a tree, like my husband found his, what do you do with that village?
Enough to say that they built for three days 130 caskets.
But that, there was no village left.
And the partisans would walk in the middle of the day, and nobody ran to the Nazis to tell them.
What was the name of the village?
I don't even know.
My husband, maybe, remembers.
I didn't know any names.
And half of it, my dear, maybe I forgot, or maybe I blanked it out.
But there was something that-- Jews, by nature, are peaceful people.
They taught us to be killers.
And they taught us never again.
Jesus said, meek will inherit the earth.
I don't see it that way.
The meek perished, and the strong are here.
I didn't.
No.
My job was to go-- every morning, I had to go to the Russian base, which was about six, seven kilometers away.
And I had to bring, in the morning, the news from Moscow.
Were given there on the radio.
It was printed up, and I brought it.
But over there, always circled German planes.
And they would shoot at-- [MAKING GUN SOUNDS].
And I went everywhere my husband went.
Thou goes, and I follow.
This was the culture, and this was the way.
I sent letters.
And also, she went out of the ghetto.
It wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] It wasn't meant.
She went out through the tunnel and was killed.
She made-- well, all of them.
Everybody did.
The Nazis came in the morning, found an empty-- an empty war camp.
There was nobody there.
This was in-- in this-- sued-- this was in the courthouse, in Navahrudak.
There were 280 people left.
And the grave across is where my sister watched my parents die, across on a hill, right across from the woods called, Horajeloff.
It's all in Navahrudak.
Yes.
I went in '69, alone.
I knew that my husband-- by that time, we knew that this husband survived and lives in Minsk.
And so does his sister.
And I have two cousins.
My mother had another rebellious sister who became a physician.
She graduated in Switzerland, in Bern.
But she went back together with Lenin.
She-- she was together with Lenin.
He was a friend.
Together with Lenin.
And her husband, Rosenberg, who was a scientist, back to Russia to Leningrad, to make the revolution and-- and build-- build a new-- a new country.
Her two sons were poisoned.
Her son-- her husband was killed.
She changed her name.
Instead-- instead of Dr.
Dara Rosenberg, she went back to her maiden name of Schlesinger.
Her daughters are two physicians.
And they said, in that country, they will never have any children.
And they don't.
But they told, to be brought here, what would they do here?
They have a cultural life there.
And-- but they were idealistic.
They believed in something.
Who knows?
And-- and we came-- the family now, from the family that is-- the old people died out.
I have still a cousin, my mother's-- there were six children.
My mother had two-- three-- there were three sisters and three brothers.
One brother survived in South America.
He had two children.
One son died already.
I know that my cousin, Rose, Rosita, is still alive.
I correspond with her in German, because she was brought up in Germany.
And she come-- came to me a few times.
Navahrudak, yes.
It was a nightmare.
Nothing is there.
Nothing was built up.
And--
Yeah, in '69.
And I was in '84.
And now, my friends-- I couldn't go-- but they went there two years ago.
Two years ago?
Or a year ago-- 1994, I think.
They all went there, and they went to our bunkers.
They found them.
There's a movie made about it.
And I couldn't go, because it was sick.
Otherwise, I was-- I would go too.
And the town-- I came to my high school in 1969, and I found the janitor.
I said, my God, he was so old.
He must have been older than I at that time, 10 years older.
How was he? 23? 24?
[LAUGHING] So of course, he was alive in '69.
So he said, hey, you are that mouse, that skinny mouse, who used the come every morning and wake me up.
I had to be the first one in school to open the class.
Nothing was the same.
I came to the graves.
They were full of-- of weeds.
Now, it's cleaned up, thanks to a friend of ours, who is in London, Jack Kagan.
They had monuments built.
When I came, the word 'Jew' wasn't mentioned.
It was only written-- the peaceful citizens of the City of Navahrudak.
He made sure that there is a Jewish star.
And it's written that the Jews are there in the grave-- Jewish people.
And nobody's left, and life goes on.
My mother was in Paris.
I don't know, exactly-- it was long, long before me.
And she found a picture of an impressionist by the name of Monet.
So she bought a picture of him-- a man and a woman, I remember, standing over a basket of potato with a little-- potatoes-- with a little child.
And they picked it-- and they are blessing it.
And that picture, my mother said, one day, is going to be worth a lot of money, so watch it.
I watched it so well that, near the signature, on the left side, I scratched it.
And I remember my mother slapped me the first time in my life over my hand.
Don't you ever touch it.
In 1959, I come to Paris with a guy-- we considers-- consider ourselves relatives.
He's the only one left from the-- the family of my aunt, and I'm the only one left of my mother's bother's family.
The only one.
We go to the Louvre.
And I stand there, and I said, Andre, doesn't this picture look like mine?
Like ours?
You remember, in mom-- he comes to it.
He said, if there is a little scratch near the signature, it's yours.
Sure enough.
That was the first thing the Nazis took off when they came into our house from the wall.
Here went my mother's fortune.
It's there.
I-- I don't know how it got there.
But it's all kinds of peculiar stories.
I'm sure my-- my parent's had money abroad.
I never remembered the number.
And I never could-- I took a lawyer, but I could never prove it.
Their signatures were forged, and the money's out.
So we started from scratch.
I found out that money can buy you comfort, but-- there is a Hebrew word, which means, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], but happiness comes from within.
And if you build your life as best as you know how, you cannot forget the past, but you try to live your life today.
And I'm speaking today, because I want to preserve my past.
Because with me-- hopefully, my daughters will remember something.
I left a book.
Hopefully, my grandchildren will know something.
But the most important thing is the museum.
And that's where it will be preserved for people to learn and to know.
Yes.
I wrote I wrote always.
I wrote for my children.
And they would grab it-- like the they would grab it, and they would read it.
So until my daughters came to me, especially my younger one, and they said, mom, why don't you publish it?
Needless to say, that to publish a book when I wanted, 15 years ago, you needed $60,000 to $70,000.
And no publishing house-- although I had a-- a-- a agent, Susan Zeckendorf-- she did it, you know, gratis, pro Bono, like, like, she didn't take money for it, or anything.
She tried to sell it, and she couldn't.
So I went to Jerusalem, and I published it.
Actually, my husband did.
We brought it here.
I spread it around the libraries and in Holocaust centers.
And it's part of the study.
And the study gives three credits, so I'm happy.
I'm happy that the good colleges have it-- Ivy colleges.
Yale has it, and it's part of the curriculum.
So it's-- I accomplished something.
You've spoken at Yale?
Yes, in 1993, in March, I spoke in Yale, in the [?
Whitten ?] Hall.
And I must say, it was-- I wasn't prepared, like I'm not prepared now.
I cannot write down and plan a-- a-- a speech.
It has come from the heart.
I'm not a-- a-- a professional speaker.
I was successful.
It was [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE]-- that's the one-- the one remainder from the Warsaw ghetto, from the resistance.
And then, I came out, and I said, there are my goodbyes.
Nobody mentions that.
I am one of them.
Maybe not a big one, but anybody, I said, anybody who even dared at the grave to speak a Nazi in the face was a hero.
Any one of those martyrs who did something was a hero.
And they are unsung heroes.
So it-- it was-- it was packed with many people.
And my younger daughter came with my grandson.
And I don't know what he expected, but he looks like he didn't expect to be right-- it-- right in the middle of-- of slum New Haven.
[LAUGHS] But he [INAUDIBLE].
Are you actively involved in Holocaust organizations?
Yes.
I am connected with the-- I was sick for a long time.
And I'm just now getting back being active.
I'm connected with Ramapo College in-- Ramapo College Holocaust Center.
And of course, everything, yearly, goes to Washington, to the museum.
And wherever there is a Holocaust Center, wherever's-- to the Anti-Defamation League, they have the Hidden Child, the hidden children that survived, I participate there too.
So wherever-- a little bit goes everywhere.
I'm active in ORT, because it teaches a profession.
I am active in Hadassah, because it has a hospital and helps the sick.
And most of all, I go as often as I can to Israel, because my friends went there and built it.
And now, there are three generations of graves.
And out of guilt, maybe, but I go there, because we are less and less, just to spend some time with those who are left.
And I bring them here.
They came to my-- our-- the weddings of the children, to the bar mitzvahs of the grandchildren, to our 50th anniversary.
It's a very close family.
And thank God that there was a Jewish Brigade.
We didn't have any mules, like the Israeli Army had with the English, when they fought with the mules, and with the Aussies, with the Australians.
But there-- there were quite a few Jews, young Jews, who didn't know any-- any-- were maybe not educated, whatever, but they knew they were the salt of the earth.
And they inherited.
And they, from them, there are many thousands others-- the children and the grandchildren now.
And some of my friends, who got married at 17, and who were-- had married children at  38, and became grandmothers at 40, so by now, we have-- they have great grandchildren.
Whoever heard living that long and seeing?
It's a privilege.
I think, if you want to ask me anything else, by all means.
The experiences-- it's a long life, and it's hard to forgive.
But we must learn not to forget, but not to live with hatred.
Mm-mm.
It destroys.
We must learn to be aware and build-- build in our children and our grandchildren the strength, because it can happen any place, at anytime, to anybody.
It's enough to look in Europe what's going on now.
And I really don't know what to think, because Tito was my hero.
He sent to us messages, arm and fight.
And the Croats and the rest fought with the Nazis, so I am completely at loss there.
And I remember, when I used to be angry at somebody, I used to say, I'll Balkanize you.
[LAUGHS] So we didn't have much hope for the Balkans.
But the Serbs were our friends.
They helped us.
Tito helped us.
And Tito said, if I'm going to build communism, Stalin, you don't interfere, and America, you don't interfere.
I'm building my own.
I know what my people need.
They need a strong hand.
And he unify-- unified them.
And now, it has fallen apart.
And then, when I look at the women and the children, I see us.
And I ache, because they are innocent.
And there's nothing I can do about it.
Nothing anybody can do about it.
They have to fight it out by themselves, because is-- this is actually like a civil war.
Try to live day to day the best way you know how, and enjoy it while it lasts.
It's darn short.
Because here I am sitting, a woman 71.
I say, what the heck?
What happened to me?
I suddenly-- suddenly, am I 71?
Where is that young girl?
It goes terribly fast.
Try to make the best and, believe that life is good.
It can be good.
It's not easy, but it can be good.
OK.
Thank you.
